comment.body:
May’s CPI came in softer than expected—with headline up 0.1% MoM vs. 0.2%
In a positive S&P 500/10-year Treasury beta regime, such a “bad surprise” drives both stocks and yields lower.
gives Fed breathing room but stickier core and tariff pass-through argue against cuts before year-end.

comment.body:
I always struggle with these two:

Coding the centroid at 6039.94 - 6053.06 zone.

Coding 5993.93 for downside pivot here, we should start to see the downside gamma manifesitng, with downside target of 5968 especially during the PM. 

Do most people use the first note to trade off of or the second? My instincts say the first one makes more sense but not entirely what the second paragraph means.

comment.body:
Alma - as always thank you for your valuable posts 

1. Weekly post gave us the range (bound by the IC). 

2. Intraday posts gave us some good levels and timing.

All very actionable 

The  spx/lty chart is a new one integrated into your analysis and I’m still trying to understand what to glean from it. But that plus your 5 yr swaps / fed funds charts are telling us a lot about the direction that the Fed may go in the future on policy. I look forward to seeing you elaborate on what you see happening after tomorrow’s PPI. 2.4% cpi vs 2.5% expected isn’t a huge variance. As you’ve been saying the
Market isn’t afraid of inflation as much as it had been. Is stagflation therefore something we’re now looking at the growth side of the equation.
comment.raw_body:

I look forward to seeing you elaborate on what you see happening after tomorrow’s PPI. 2.4% cpi vs 2.5% expected isn’t a huge variance. As you’ve been saying the
Market isn’t afraid of inflation as much as it had been. Is stagflation therefore something we’re now looking at the growth side of the equation.
